literal: from √bṛh, 'to grow, to swell, to expand'; the swelling-without-bound, the encompassing reality
invariant_definition: The ultimate referent of Upaniṣadic inquiry and the target of *brahma-jijñāsā*. In Vedānta the term does not merely mean a highest being; it names that from which the world arises, by which it is sustained, into which it resolves, and on knowing which the bondage of *saṃsāra* is ended. The term therefore sits inside a technical matrix: *jagat-kāraṇatva*, debates over *upādāna* and *nimitta-kāraṇa*, the *sat-kārya* question of whether effects pre-exist in their cause, the opposition of *nirguṇa* and *saguṇa*, and the relation between *brahman*, *īśvara*, *paramātman*, and *bhagavān*. All Vedāntic schools agree that *brahman* is the purport of the Upaniṣads and the reality marked by *janmādy asya yataḥ*; they disagree on whether the world is a real mode, a real power, or an apparent manifestation of that reality, and on whether differentiation belongs to *brahman* itself or only to what depends on it. In practice the term also governs *upāsanā*: the realized object of liberating knowledge for Advaita, and the real Lord to be known, served, and loved for the theistic schools.

school_framing.shared_core: Within Vedānta, *brahman* names **the ultimate referent of Upaniṣadic inquiry: that from which the world arises, by which it is sustained, into which it resolves, and on knowing which saṃsāra is ended** (Taittirīya 3.1.1; *janmādy asya yataḥ*, Brahma-Sūtra 1.1.2). Every Vedāntic school agrees that *brahman* is the purport of the Upaniṣads and the *jagat-kāraṇa*. They differ on whether *brahman* is *nirviśeṣa* (Advaita) or *saviśeṣa* (Viśiṣṭādvaita, Tattva-vāda, Gauḍīya, Nimbārka, Vallabha), and on how *jīva* and world stand to it (apparent appearance, real mode, real *aṃśa*, real *pariṇāma*, *acintya-bhedābheda*).
school_framing.register_axes_note: Vedāntic schools speak in the same M-register about the same scope-inside (*jagat-kāraṇa*, *jñeya*); they genuinely disagree (AF1) on whether differentiation belongs to *brahman* itself. Non-Vedāntic appearances are partial or *different concept*: Mīmāṃsā's *brahman*-language is ritual-priestly, not metaphysical; Sāṅkhya commentary glosses it as *avyakta/prakṛti*; Buddhists have no *brahman* as substrate but use *paramārtha/tathatā* as functional analogues.

per_school.definition: Brahman is articulated as genuinely two-formed, *mūrta* and *amūrta*, and the subsequent *neti neti* functions as a delimitative excess over predication rather than as a retraction of that articulation [[BṛU 2.3.1, 2.3.6](cite://bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-fragments/2.3)]. The *mahāvākya* *ahaṃ brahmāsmi* is therefore preserved as a real identity-statement, not reduced to a merely figurative *upāsanā-vākya* [[BṛU 1.4.10](cite://bhartrprapanca/brhadaranyaka-commentary-fragments/1.4.10)].

Brahman is *ātmaprakāśa* and *ānanda-svabhāva*, with finite pleasures registering only as delimited measures of that plenitude [[BS 1.1 (opening ānanda discussion)](cite://mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.1--opening-prose-discussion-of-nanda)]. The apprehension of plurality is *māyā-nibandhana*, and *avidyā* is neither *brahma-svabhāva* nor a sheer non-entity but an *anirvacanīya mithyāvabhāsa* whose locus is the *jīvas* [[BS 1.1 (plurality as māyā-bound)](cite://mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.1--opening-prose-on-ekatva-and-m-y); [BS 1.1 (avidyā neither sat nor asat; in the jīvas)](cite://mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.1--opening-prose-on-avidy)]. Accordingly *kāraṇa-brahman* is established as *anirvacanīya-māyā-viśiṣṭa*, and the *mahāvākya* yields *aparokṣa* only through the aid of *manana* and allied disciplines, so that *brahma-vidyā* is the *nirvikalpaka mano-vṛtti* whose object is the *advitīyātman* [[PV p. 212](cite://prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/brahmaṇo lakṣaṇopalakṣaṇe); [PV p. 103](cite://prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/mahāvākyasyāparokṣa-prayojakatvam); [BR 1.1 ṭīkā](cite://madhusudana/bhakti-rasayana/1.ṭīkā.1)].
primary_loci: Brahma-Siddhi 1.1 (avidyā neither sat nor asat; in the jīvas)

citations.locus_short: BS 1.1 (avidyā neither sat nor asat; in the jīvas)

per_school.register_tag: M+E; mumukṣu [REAL-DISAGREEMENT vs. Viśiṣṭādvaita/Tattva-vāda on nirviśeṣatva]

per_school.definition: By the word *brahman* one intends *Puruṣottama*, intrinsically free of every *doṣa* and possessed of *ananta-kalyāṇa-guṇa*, so that the *brahma-śabda* takes its primary force in the *sarveśvara* himself [[Śrī-Bhāṣya, Commentary 6](cite://ramanuja/shri-bhasya/6); [Śrī-Bhāṣya, Commentary 11](cite://ramanuja/shri-bhasya/11)]. This *brahman* is Nārāyaṇa as *antaryāmin*, the single cause of the *cit-acit* cosmos and the inner self of all other entities, which stand to him through *śakti*-, *aṃśa*-, *vibhūti*-, *rūpa*-, and *śarīra*-vācaka expressions in *sāmānādhikaraṇya* [[Vedārtha-Saṅgraha §6](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§6)].
per_school.register_tag: M+S; prapanna [REAL-DISAGREEMENT vs. Advaita on saviśeṣatva]

per_school.definition: Brahman is Viṣṇu, *nirdoṣa*, *nityapūrṇa* in all *guṇas*, and the unique *svatantra* under whose *vaśa* all other beings stand [[VTV 3.1](cite://madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.1); [VTV 3.2](cite://madhva/visnu-tattva-vinirnaya/3.2)]. For the *mumukṣu* *brahman* alone is the proper *jñeya*, and *ānanda*-language is read through an explicit hierarchy in which finite enjoyers are delimited while the *ānandamaya* bears fullness without remainder [[TD 1.4](cite://raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.4); [TD 1.1](cite://raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.1)].
per_school.register_tag: M+S; Tattvavādin [REAL-DISAGREEMENT vs. Advaita on jīveśvara-bheda]

per_school.definition: The *abheda-śāstras* concerning *brahman* are retained at the level of shared *cid-rūpatva*, while the *jīva-samūha* stands to Bhagavān through his *svābhāvika acintya-śakti* as ray-atoms stand to the sun, so that *vyatireka* and *avyatireka* are jointly upheld [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §43](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§43)]. Once the distinction between the *māyā-āśraya* and the *māyā-mohita* is fixed, *bhajana* of him alone becomes *abhidheya* and Bhagavān is established as the *parama-svarūpa* and proper *viṣaya* of *parama-prema* [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §44](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§44); [Paramātma-Sandarbha §45](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§45)].

*Parabrahman* is Kṛṣṇa/Hari, *svabhāvataḥ apāsta-samasta-doṣa* and the *eka-rāśi* of *aśeṣa-kalyāṇa-guṇas* [[DaŚ 4](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/4)]. *Tattva* is triadic as *cit*, *acit*, and *brahman*, with *brahman* and *jīva* standing as *svādhīna* and *parādhīna* [[Vedānta-Kaustubha, BS 1.1.1](cite://srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/1.1.1)]. The *jīva* is neither *atyantabhinna* nor *atyantābhinna*, but *brahman*'s *aṃśa* understood as *śakti* and hence real non-separation with real distinction [[Vedānta-Kaustubha, BS 2.3.42](cite://srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/2.3.42); [VPS 2.3.42](cite://nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/2.3.42)].

Brahman itself is the *samavāyi-kāraṇa*, pervading its effects as *sat-cit-ānanda* while *nāma* and *rūpa* belong to the *kārya*-side [[BSB 1.1.4](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.4)]. Its manifestation is a real *pariṇāma* in *kāryākāra*, yet *avikṛta* in the manner of gold becoming ornaments, so that *brahman*'s plenitude is not diminished by emergence [[BSB 1.4.26](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.4.26)]. The *jīva* is *brahmaṇo 'ṃśaḥ*, and *brahmajñāna* matures into *Puruṣottama-prāpti* only through the *bhakti-bhāva* that renders the soul fit for divine appropriation [[BSB 2.3.43-45](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/2.3.43-45); [BSB 1.1.12-19](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.12-19)].

Where brahma-language appears in the Sāṅkhya commentarial corpus, it is glossed as avyakta/prakṛti, the bahudhātmaka unmanifest source from which mahat and the later evolutes proceed [[SK 22 comm.](cite://mathara/sankhya-karika-vrtti/22)]. Liberation therefore lies not in identity with a single brahman but in aikāntika, ātyantika kaivalya, attained when pradhāna has completed its puruṣārtha and no further body is produced for the knower [[SK 68 comm.](cite://mathara/sankhya-karika-vrtti/68)].

Yoga does not install brahman as an undifferentiated world-ground; its highest positive principle is Īśvara, the puruṣa-viśeṣa eternally untouched by kleśa, karma, vipāka, and āśaya, and uniquely marked by unsurpassable sovereignty and omniscience [[YBh 1.24-1.25](cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/1.24-1.25)]. When the bhāṣya uses brahma-language, it does so within this psychology of witnessing consciousness: the sages discern the eternal brahma hidden in the cave in connection with the buddhivṛtti through which the unchanging seer is made manifest [[YBh 4.22](cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/4.22)].

In the Bhikṣu synthesis, *brahman* is not approached through an epistemic thinning of *māyā* into mere *avidyā*; in *Śvetāśvatara* 4.10 *māyā* is read as *prakṛti* itself, preserving a real causal apparatus for manifestation [[VĀB 1.1.5](cite://vijnanabhiksu/vijnanamrta-bhasya/1.1.5)]. The *jīva* is *brahmaṇo 'ṃśaḥ* in an expressive and dependent sense, like a son to a father, and yogic *īśvara-praṇidhāna* is accordingly a *samādhi-bhāvanā* oriented to a *puruṣa-viśeṣa* untouched by *kleśa-karma-vipākāśaya* [[VĀB 2.3.43](cite://vijnanabhiksu/vijnanamrta-bhasya/2.3.43); [YV 1.23](cite://vijnanabhiksu/yoga-varttika/1.23); [YV 1.24](cite://vijnanabhiksu/yoga-varttika/1.24)].

Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika's functional equivalent to brahman is Īśvara, not an undifferentiated absolute: the Lord is posited as cause because human action by itself does not account for the fruition of karmic results [[NS 4.1.19](cite://gautama/nyaya-sutra/4.1.19)]. Liberation, however, is defined as apavarga, the utter cessation of duḥkha and rebirth, which Vātsyāyana can describe as the fearless and deathless brahma-kṣemaprāpti [[NB 1.1.22](cite://vatsyayana/nyaya-bhasya/1.1.22)].

per_school.not_applicable_reason: In the on-disk Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā corpus, brahman-language is used for the Brahman priest, brahmasāman, or other ritual designata; the school's autonomous topic is dharma/apūrva rather than brahman as an ultimate reality.

per_school.definition: Mādhyamaka does not replace the Upaniṣadic brahman with another self-existent substrate; its functional analogue to ultimacy is paramārtha, taught only in dependence on saṃvṛti and reached only by passing through it [[MMK 24.8-10](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.8-10)]. That paramārtha is dharmatā: unproduced, unceased, peaceful, free from prapañca and conceptual bifurcation, and Candrakīrti accordingly glosses nirvāṇa's very emptiness as pāramārthikī śūnyatā [[MMK 18.7-9](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/18.7-9); [MAv 6.189-190](cite://candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/6.189-190)].
per_school.register_tag: LD+M; reflective reader [no brahman; functional analogue paramārtha/tathatā]

per_school.definition: Yogācāra's closest analogue to brahman is not a permanent substance but the paramārtha of dharmas as tathatā, which Vasubandhu identifies with vijñaptimātratā itself [[Trimsikā 23-28](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/23-28)]. The school therefore dissolves grāhya-grāhaka duality rather than uncovering a self-existent ground: vijñaptimātra is taught so that one enters dharma-nairātmya by seeing that the imagined svabhāva of object and subject is merely kalpita [[Viṃśatikā 10](cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/10)].

Pratyabhijñā / Trika can use brahman-language, but it identifies the highest referent as a luminous, pure, peaceful nondual whole that rests in śakti rather than as a quiescent neuter absolute [[Paramārtha-Sāra 43](cite://abhinavagupta/paramartha-sara/43)]. Accordingly the tradition criticizes a merely brahmarūpa prakāśamātra devoid of vimarśa and svātantrya as inert; only consciousness endowed with spanda and self-referential lordship is fully adequate to the real [[Spanda comm. 1.5.11-14](cite://ksemaraja/spanda-karika-commentary/1.5.11-14)].

textual_history.summary: The career of *brahman* runs from a concrete ritual term to the name of the Absolute, and the jump is real. In the Ṛgveda *bráhman* (neuter, from √bṛh 'to swell, make grow') is the sacred formula, the inspired chant or mantra, the soul-power of the uttered Word that swells the gods and the sacrifice — something the poet *fashions* as a craftsman fashions a chariot (RV 5.29.15). Aurobindo, reading the Veda for its inner sense, glosses the word exactly so: 'Brahman in the Veda signifies ordinarily the Vedic Word or mantra in its profoundest aspect as the expression of the intuition arising out of the depths of the soul or being' (Aurobindo, *The Secret of the Veda*); his is an interpretive lens, but it tracks the plain ritual usage of *bráhman* as the formula-power. By the late-Vedic and Upaniṣadic period the term has migrated to the unconditioned reality itself — that from which beings arise, by which they live, into which they return (Taittirīya 3.1.1), defined as 'the real, knowledge, the infinite' (Taittirīya 2.1.1), and declared in the great identity-sentences to be all this (Chāndogya 3.14.1) and one's own self (Chāndogya 6.8.7; Bṛhadāraṇyaka 1.4.10). The *Bhagavad-Gītā* inherits this absolute sense, naming *brahman* the imperishable and the state one 'becomes' (8.3; 14.27). The connective tissue between the two poles is partly genealogical (the same word, carried by the same priestly-Upaniṣadic transmission) and partly a matter of how the tradition itself read its inheritance: the Upaniṣads do not gloss their *brahman* by quoting the Ṛgvedic formula, so the move from 'formula' to 'Absolute' is a documented semantic shift, not a chain of explicit citation. Where the link is resonance rather than textual genealogy, that is marked in the notes below.

stages.sanskrit: indra brahma kriyamāṇā juṣasva yā te śaviṣṭha navyā akarma | vastreva bhadrā sukṛtā vasūyū rathaṁ na dhīraḥ svapā atakṣam
stages.rendering: Indra, take delight in the sacred formulae (brahma) being made — the new ones we have made for you, mightiest. Like fair, well-wrought garments, desiring wealth, as a skilled craftsman fashions a chariot, I have fashioned [them].
stages.note: The earliest pole: *bráhman* is the inspired ritual utterance, here explicitly something *made* and *fashioned* (atakṣam, √takṣ 'to chisel, carpenter') like a chariot. It is the soul-power of the chanted Word, not yet a metaphysical absolute. Aurobindo's reading — *bráhman* as 'the Vedic Word or mantra in its profoundest aspect' (*The Secret of the Veda*) — is his interpretive lens on exactly this usage; what the text plainly gives is the formula-power that the priest crafts and Indra is asked to enjoy. Cf. RV 1.10.1 (brahmāṇas tvā … ud vaṁśam iva yemire, 'the chanters have raised you up as [men raise] a beam') and RV 10.71, on the fashioning of the sacred Word.

stages.sanskrit: sarvaṃ khalv idaṃ brahma tajjalān iti śānta upāsīta | atha khalu kratumayaḥ puruṣo yathākratur asmiṃl loke puruṣo bhavati tathetaḥ pretya bhavati | sa kratuṃ kurvīta
stages.rendering: All this, truly, is brahman — as that-from-which-it-is-born, that-into-which-it-dissolves, that-by-which-it-breathes (tajjalān); so let one worship, at peace. Now, a person is made of resolve (kratu): as is one's resolve in this world, so does one become on departing hence; therefore let one form resolve.
stages.note: The semantic jump is now complete: *brahman* is no longer a formula but the all-encompassing reality, 'all this' (sarvaṃ idaṃ). The cryptic *tajjalān* is the traditional triad — that from which beings are born (ja), into which they dissolve (la), by which they breathe/live (an) — the same causal signature that Taittirīya 3.1 spells out and that Bādarāyaṇa fixes as *janmādy asya yataḥ*. Nothing in the verse cites or echoes the Ṛgvedic formula-sense; the migration from 'sacred utterance' to 'world-ground' is a shift the texts enact rather than narrate.

stages.locus: Taittirīya 2.1.1 and 3.1.1 (= GRETIL TaittU 2.1.1, 3.1.1; saved at data/sources/sanskrit/vedic/taittiriya_upanisad_gretil.txt)
stages.sanskrit: satyaṃ jñānam anantaṃ brahma | yo veda nihitaṃ guhāyāṃ parame vyoman | … || (3.1.1) yato vā imāni bhūtāni jāyante | yena jātāni jīvanti | yat prayanty abhisaṃviśanti | tad vijijñāsasva | tad brahmeti
stages.rendering: Brahman is the real, knowledge, the infinite (satyaṃ jñānam anantam). Whoever knows it as set down in the secret place (guhā), in the highest heaven … (3.1.1) That from which these beings are born, by which, being born, they live, into which on departing they enter — seek to know That: That is brahman.
stages.note: The two definitions that the later Vedānta treats as the standard *svarūpa-* and *taṭastha-lakṣaṇa* of brahman. 2.1.1 gives the essential character ('the real, knowledge, the infinite'); 3.1.1 (Bhṛgu's instruction by Varuṇa) gives the causal mark — origin, sustenance, and dissolution of all beings — which Brahma-Sūtra 1.1.2 (*janmādy asya yataḥ*) crystallises. The 'secret place' (guhā) of 2.1.1 is the same hidden interior where the Ṛgveda lodges Agni; here it houses brahman itself.

stages.locus: Bṛhadāraṇyaka 1.4.10 (= GRETIL BrhUp 1,4.10; saved at data/sources/sanskrit/vedic/brhadaranyaka_upanisad_gretil.txt); Chāndogya 6.8.7 (ChUp 6,8.7)
stages.sanskrit: brahma vā idam agra āsīt | tad ātmānam evāvet | ahaṃ brahmāsmīti | tasmāt tat sarvam abhavat || (ChU 6.8.7) sa ya eṣo 'ṇimaitad ātmyam idaṃ sarvam | tat satyam | sa ātmā | tat tvam asi śvetaketo iti
stages.rendering: Brahman, truly, was this in the beginning. It knew only itself: 'I am brahman.' Therefore it became all. || (ChU 6.8.7) That which is this finest essence — this whole world has that as its self. That is the real. That is the self. That you are, Śvetaketu.
stages.note: The *mahāvākyas*: brahman is now identified with the self and with all that is. *ahaṃ brahmāsmi* ('I am brahman') and *tat tvam asi* ('that you are') make the absolute and the *ātman* co-referential — the move on which the whole later *brahman*/*ātman* equation rests (see the ātman entry's textual_history for the converse arc). Note that the schools divide sharply on how literally to take these identity-sentences (see per_school above); the textual datum is only that the Upaniṣads state the identity.

stages.locus: Bhagavad-Gītā 8.3 and 14.27 (verbatim from the embedded Gītā verses in data/sources/sanskrit/vedanta/shankara_gita_bhasya.txt)
stages.sanskrit: akṣaraṃ brahma paramaṃ svabhāvo 'dhyātmam ucyate | … (14.27) brahmaṇo hi pratiṣṭhāham amṛtasyāvyayasya ca | śāśvatasya ca dharmasya sukhasyaikāntikasya ca
stages.rendering: The supreme imperishable is brahman; its own-being is called the over-self (adhyātma) … (14.27) For I am the foundation of brahman — of the immortal and the imperishable, of the eternal law, and of absolute bliss.
stages.note: The *Gītā* receives the Upaniṣadic absolute sense fully: brahman is *akṣara*, the imperishable (8.3), and the state one 'becomes' — *brahma-bhūta* (5.24, the same usage the brahmanirvana entry tracks) — knowledge of which ends becoming. The Ṛgvedic formula-sense has dropped away entirely; what survives is *brahman* as the unconditioned reality. 14.27 is doctrinally loaded — Kṛṣṇa makes himself the 'foundation of brahman', a verse the theistic schools press hard against the Advaitic reading; we cite it for the absolute sense of the term, not to adjudicate that dispute.

translator_note: Avoid lazy equivalences such as 'the Absolute' or 'God' without qualification. 'The Absolute' imports German-idealist baggage; 'God' imports a personal creator model that fits some Vedāntic schools and not others. The Sanskrit term carries a causal and soteriological apparatus: world-ground, object of liberating knowledge, and, in many schools, the real Lord of worship. Also keep the distinctions clear: *brahman* (neuter) is not *brahmā* (the creator deity) and not *brāhmaṇa* (the ritual-social designation).
